text: Welcome to 6 Minute English from BBC Learning English.
zh: 欢迎收听BBC英语教学的《六分钟英语》节目。

text: I'm Bekkah. And I'm Georgie. Remember, you can find all this episode's vocabulary along
zh: 我是Bekkah。我是Georgie。记得可以在我们的网站bbclearningenglish.com上找到本期节目的所有词汇、文字稿和练习题。

text: with a transcript and worksheet on our website bbclearningenglish
zh: （同上，已合并）

text: .com. Now, Bekkah, do you read a lot? Hmm, I don't read often. I just feel like I don't have the time, Georgie.
zh: 那么，Bekkah，你经常读书吗？嗯，我不常读书，我总觉得自己没时间，Georgie。

text: How about yourself? I would love to read more, but I don't read very much at the moment.
zh: 你呢？我很想多读点书，但目前读得不多。

text: I read mostly before bed because I feel like it helps me relax and go to sleep.
zh: 我通常在睡前读书，因为我觉得这能帮助我放松、入睡。

text: And today we're talking all about reading. We'll be hearing from some experts about how reading can change our brains.
zh: 今天我们要聊的话题就是阅读。我们会听听专家们讲阅读如何改变我们的大脑。

text: And as usual, we'll be learning some useful new words and phrases.
zh: 像往常一样，我们也会学到一些实用的新单词和短语。

text: Let's start with a quiz question. The longest novel in the world is widely thought to be by French author Marcel Proust.
zh: 我们先从一道测验题开始吧。人们普遍认为世界上最长的小说是法国作家马塞尔·普鲁斯特写的。

text: A book which, when translated into English, means remembrance of things past. But how many words does the book contain?
zh: 这本书翻译成英文后书名意为《追忆逝水年华》。那么这本书一共有多少个单词呢？

text: Is it a 130 ,000, b 1 .3 million or c 13 million?
Okay, the longest novel in the world. I still think 13 million words sounds too many.
zh: 好吧，世界上最长的小说。我还是觉得1300万个单词听起来太多了。

text: So I'm going to go with B, 1 .3 million. All right, we'll find out at the end of the program.
zh: 所以我选B，130万。好，我们节目最后会揭晓答案。

text: Now, we might think of reading as like speaking. We're born with the potential to do it, and then we learn.
zh: 我们可能会认为阅读就像说话一样，是我们天生就具备的能力，然后再去学习。

text: It's natural. If something is natural, it's something you were born with or that comes from nature.
zh: 这是自然而然的。如果某事是“natural（自然的）”，意味着它是你与生俱来的，或者是天性使然。

text: But Marianne Wolfe, author of the book Reader Come Home, says that this isn't true.
zh: 但《读者归来》一书的作者玛丽安·沃尔夫说，事实并非如此。

text: We think of language as natural, and reading is written language, so it must be natural.
zh: 我们认为语言是自然的，而阅读是书面语言，所以它也一定是自然的。

text: But it isn't. It isn't natural at all.
zh: 但其实不是，阅读一点也不自然。

text: Scientific studies suggest that when we're born, our
zh: 科学研究表明，我们出生时，

text: brains already have the networks that allow our eyes to see and our vocal cords to produce sounds,
zh: 大脑中已经具备了让眼睛看见事物、声带发出声音的神经网络，

text: but not with the pathways we need to read.
zh: 但却没有阅读所需要的神经通路。

text: Let's hear more from psychologist and neuroscientist Rebecca Gottlieb, speaking to the BBC World Service.
zh: 让我们听听心理学家兼神经科学家丽贝卡·戈特利布在接受BBC世界服务采访时怎么说。

text: From an evolutionary timescale, our brain hasn't had enough time to develop
zh: 从进化的时间尺度来看，我们的大脑还没有足够的时间发展出

text: a dedicated reading brain. And so to build a reading brain network,
zh: 专门用于阅读的大脑区域。因此，为了构建阅读所需的大脑网络，

text: we co -op parts of the brain involved in vision and auditory processing and language and attention and
zh: 我们会借用大脑中原本负责视觉、听觉处理、语言以及注意力和

text: affect. Reading is really a whole brain process.
zh: 情感的部分。阅读其实是一个调动全脑的过程。

text: It involves activation in all four lobes of the cortex.
zh: 它涉及大脑皮层四个脑叶的全部激活。

text: The process of developing a reading brain alters everything from
zh: 阅读大脑发展的过程会改变从

text: brain activity to brain structure and brain connectivity.
zh: 大脑活动到大脑结构，再到大脑连接方式的方方面面。

text: The power of deep reading is really fundamental to our humanity.
zh: 深度阅读的力量对我们的人性来说其实是根本性的。

text: When we read deeply, we change our brains and we change who we are.
zh: 当我们深度阅读时，我们改变了自己的大脑，也改变了我们自己。

text: Rebecca says that our brains haven't evolved to include a dedicated reading brain.
zh: 丽贝卡说，我们的大脑并没有进化出专门负责阅读的区域。

text: Dedicated here means designed and used for one particular purpose.
zh: 这里的“dedicated（专用的）”指专门为某一特定目的而设计和使用的。

text: So, because we don't have a part of the brain designed specifically for reading, when we learn to read we co -opt other
zh: 所以，正因为大脑中没有专门为阅读设计的区域，我们学习阅读时就会借用大脑的其他

text: parts of the brain. Co -opt here means to include someone or something, often against their will.
zh: 部分。这里的“co-opt（借用/征用）”指纳入某人或某物，往往是在其不情愿的情况下。

text: Right. Learning to read means using lots of different parts of the brain that are designed for other things,
zh: 没错。学习阅读意味着使用大脑中许多原本为其他功能设计的部分，

text: and this changes our brain structure compared to someone who hasn't learned to read. And the language we read also shapes our brain.
zh: 这会使我们的大脑结构与没有学过阅读的人有所不同。而我们所阅读的语言本身，也会塑造我们的大脑。

text: Chinese characters, for example, use symbols instead of letters of the alphabet to represent words and ideas.
zh: 比如说，汉字使用的是符号，而不是字母表中的字母，来表示词语和概念。

text: Research suggests that learning to read these symbols activates different areas of the brain to reading an alphabet -based system.
zh: 研究表明，学习阅读这些符号所激活的大脑区域，与阅读以字母为基础的文字系统时所激活的区域不同。

text: Scientists studied a bilingual man who could read and speak Chinese and English.
zh: 科学家们研究了一位既能读又能说中文和英文的双语男子。

text: The man suffered a stroke which affected parts of his brain, including his ability to read Chinese.
zh: 这名男子中风后，大脑的某些部分受到影响，其中包括阅读中文的能力。

text: But amazingly, he was still able to read English.
zh: 但令人惊讶的是，他仍然能够阅读英文。

text: Marianne Wolfe explains more to the BBC World Service.
zh: 玛丽安·沃尔夫在接受BBC世界服务采访时做了进一步解释。

text: It's a beautiful example of how the brain's circuit
zh: 这是一个很好的例子，说明大脑的回路

text: reflects the requirements environments of Chinese,
zh: 是如何反映中文这种环境所提出的要求的，

text: which inevitably means more visual memory
zh: 这不可避免地意味着需要更多的视觉记忆，

text: and visual processing of those beautifully intricate symbols or characters.
zh: 以及对那些精美复杂的符号——也就是汉字——进行更多的视觉处理。

text: Marianne says that the brain circuit is shaped by learning to read Chinese. A circuit is a system of connections.
zh: 玛丽安说，学习阅读中文会塑造大脑的回路。“circuit（回路）”指的是一种连接系统。

text: The visual qualities of Chinese symbols inevitably mean more visual areas of the brain are developed.
zh: 汉字的视觉特性不可避免地意味着大脑中更多与视觉相关的区域会得到发展。

text: Inevitably means in a way that cannot be stopped or avoided.
zh: “inevitably（不可避免地）”意思是以一种无法阻止或避免的方式。

text: Mary -Anne describes the symbolic Chinese characters as beautifully intricate.
zh: 玛丽安将象形的汉字描述为“beautifully intricate（精美而复杂）”。

text: If something is intricate, it has lots of detail. And something which also has lots of detail, or certainly lots of words,
zh: 如果某样东西是“intricate（复杂精细的）”，那它就包含很多细节。而另一样同样细节丰富——或者说，字数肯定很多的东西，

text: I asked you, Georgie, how many words are in Marcel Proust's Remembrance of Things Past?
zh: 我曾问过你，Georgie，马塞尔·普鲁斯特的《追忆逝水年华》一共有多少个单词？

text: I said 1 .3 million. And you were correct!
zh: 我说是130万。而你答对了！

text: Yay! The book also contains lots of very long sentences, including one with over 900 words.
zh: 太棒了！这本书里还有很多非常长的句子，其中有一句竟然超过900个单词。

text: One sentence with 900 words, that is a lot. OK, it's time to recap the language we learned during this programme,
zh: 一句话900个单词，那可真不少。好了，现在是时候回顾一下我们在这期节目中学到的词汇了，

text: starting with natural, which describes something you were born with or that comes from nature.
zh: 首先是“natural（自然的）”，指你与生俱来的，或者源自天性的东西。

text: Dedicated can describe something that is designed and used for one particular purpose.
zh: “dedicated（专用的）”可以用来形容专门为某一特定目的而设计和使用的事物。

text: If you co -opt someone or something, you involve them, sometimes against their will.
zh: 如果你“co-opt”某人或某物，就是把他们纳入进来，有时是违背其意愿的。

text: A circuit is a system of connections, for example, in the brain.
zh: “circuit（回路）”指的是一种连接系统，比如大脑中的神经回路。

text: Inevitably means in a way that cannot be stopped or avoided.
zh: “inevitably（不可避免地）”意思是以一种无法阻止或避免的方式。

text: And intricate describes something which has lots of detail. That's it for this episode of 6 Minute English.
zh: 而“intricate（复杂精细的）”则用来形容细节丰富的事物。这就是本期《六分钟英语》节目的全部内容。

text: Test what you've learnt with the worksheet on our website bbclearningenglish .com. Thanks for joining us. Goodbye. Bye.
zh: 快去我们的网站bbclearningenglish.com上用练习题检验一下你学到的内容吧。感谢大家收听，再见。再见。
